question: Where does FastAPI expose the generated OpenAPI document and its two documentation UIs?
explanation: FastAPI serves the spec at /openapi.json, interactive Swagger UI at /docs, and a clean ReDoc reference at /redoc — all generated from your route signatures and Pydantic models.

question: Beyond documentation, why does declaring response_model on a FastAPI route matter?
options: It filters the outgoing payload to the declared schema, so internal fields can't leak
It is required for async routes
explanation: response_model both documents and filters the response — FastAPI strips any field not in the declared schema. Without it, stray internal model fields (password hashes, internal flags) can leak to clients.

question: Why should even code-first teams snapshot /openapi.json in the repo and diff it in CI?
options: So an accidental breaking change (renamed field, narrowed type) fails review loudly instead of shipping silently
explanation: Treating the generated spec as a committed contract means any change to the public surface shows up as a reviewable diff. A silent breaking change (renaming a field, tightening a type) then fails CI instead of reaching clients.

question: When is contract-first (hand-written spec, then implement) the better workflow?
options: When multiple teams or external partners consume the API and the contract is the coordination artifact
explanation: Contract-first shines when the spec is the agreement between separate teams or external consumers — they can build against it in parallel before the server exists. Code-first (generate the spec from code) fits fast-moving internal APIs where drift risk is lower.
